coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous removal of heavy metal ions using carbon dots-doped hydrogel particles
coredata.dc:creator: Perumal, Suguna
Atchudan, Raji
Thirukumaran, Periyasamy
Yoon, Dong Ho
Lee, Yong Rok
Cheong, In Woo
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ions (HMI) have attracted worldwide concern due to their serious environmental pollution which led to the risk of health conditions. From Red Malus floribunda fruits, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared, followed by hybrid-spherical shaped hydrogel particles (CGCDs) were prepared. The prepared CGCDs were utilized as adsorbents for HMI-(Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III)) from water. N-CDs with about 4.0 nm in diameter were characterized by various techniques such as field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and attenuated total reflection-fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) that confirm the presence of nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon functionalities. The prepared spherical CGCDs were characterized very well before it was used as HMI adsorbents. The sizes of the CGCDs were ranges between 20 and 300 μm and the degree of swelling was calculated as 1320 %. ATR-FTIR and X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the presence of N-CDs in CGCDs. Further, FE-SEM confirms the spherical shape morphology of CGCDs. Three different concentrations of HMI solutions were 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L. Hg(II) adsorbed proficiently by CGCDs in single metal ion systems with ~72 % and almost complete removal of Hg(II) ions (99 %) in multiple metal ion systems was observed. Moreover, all metal ions Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III) were efficiently (>70 %) removed in multiple systems by CGCDs. After HMI adsorption experiments, the elemental mapping from FE-SEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy studies conveys the presence of HMI on CGCDs. This suggests that CGCDs would be a suitable adsorbent for the simultaneous removal of multiple HMI from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous removal of heavy metal ions using carbon dots-doped hydrogel particles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ions (HMI) have attracted worldwide concern due to their serious environmental pollution which led to the risk of health conditions. From Red Malus floribunda fruits, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared, followed by hybrid-spherical shaped hydrogel particles (CGCDs) were prepared. The prepared CGCDs were utilized as adsorbents for HMI-(Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III)) from water. N-CDs with about 4.0 nm in diameter were characterized by various techniques such as field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and attenuated total reflection-fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) that confirm the presence of nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon functionalities. The prepared spherical CGCDs were characterized very well before it was used as HMI adsorbents. The sizes of the CGCDs were ranges between 20 and 300 μm and the degree of swelling was calculated as 1320 %. ATR-FTIR and X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the presence of N-CDs in CGCDs. Further, FE-SEM confirms the spherical shape morphology of CGCDs. Three different concentrations of HMI solutions were 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L. Hg(II) adsorbed proficiently by CGCDs in single metal ion systems with ~72 % and almost complete removal of Hg(II) ions (99 %) in multiple metal ion systems was observed. Moreover, all metal ions Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III) were efficiently (>70 %) removed in multiple systems by CGCDs. After HMI adsorption experiments, the elemental mapping from FE-SEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy studies conveys the presence of HMI on CGCDs. This suggests that CGCDs would be a suitable adsorbent for the simultaneous removal of multiple HMI from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are natural constituents of the earth's crust, but this leads to an imbalance in human activities (Tchounwou et al., 2012; Burakov et al., 2018). Accumulation of toxic metal ions pollutes the environment and ecosystem (Ahmad et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2020; Obasi and Akudinobi, 2020; Ranjbar Jafarabadi et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2021). The risk of public health is directed to intensive studies towards water purifications. There are many processes available to remove heavy metal ions (HMI) from wastewater (Rengaraj et al., 2001; Benito and Ruíz, 2002; Yurlova et al., 2002; Priyadharsan et al., 2017). Among the existing methods, the adsorption processes have evolved as a prominent method for removing pollutants from the water system (Karthikeyan et al., 2013; Xia et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). This method is very effective in the removal of organic and inorganic pollutants in waterway systems (De Gisi et al., 2016). The adsorption of HMI with a solid surface is an effective method. Activated carbon is considered to be an efficient material to absorb the HMI from the water (Kharrazi et al., 2020). However, the cost of the production of carbon is high which led to indeed in search of alternative adsorbent material. The adsorbent should be from local natural materials so that we can overcome the cost-effectiveness.

Several studies have been conducted on the use of low-cost, biomass-derived adsorbents for the removal of HMI. A typical example can be a biopolymer-based HMI adsorbent such as chitosan and gelatin (Rinaudo, 2006; Monier and Abdel-Latif, 2012; Abdelwahab et al., 2016; Godiya et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2019c, 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d; Lone et al., 2019; Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). Chitosan is a biodegradable and biocompatible polymer which upon deacetylation led to chitin (Rinaudo, 2006). Chitosan with glutamic was tested for the removal of Cu (II) and Ni (II) from wastewater (Abdelwahab et al., 2016). Using chitosan-phenylthiourea resin, Hg (II) and Cd(II) metal ions were removed (Monier and Abdel-Latif, 2012). Though many reports available regarding the removal of HMI from the wastewater system. Still, research is focused on the formulation of an effective adsorbent for the simultaneous removal of multiple HMI ions. Recently we reported adsorbents for HMI (Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III)) using chitosan and gelatin (CG) (Lone et al., 2019; Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). Spherical hydrogel particles CG were used as adsorbents for HMI, absorbing up to 95 % of Hg(II) ions (Perumal et al., 2019). Since graphene has a high surface ratio, graphene oxide (GO) was embedded in hydrogel particles (CGGO) and used for HMI adsorption. This CGGO showed maximum adsorption of 92 % Hg(II) ions (Perumal et al., 2020). A low adsorption level of metal ions by CGGO advises searching for an alternative material with nitrogen-rich which might increase the adsorption efficiency of HMI.

Therefore we thought of enduring the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) in hydrogel particles. CDs are novel fluorescent materials, zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with a size less than 10 nm (Wang et al., 2020). CDs attracts great attention since it discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004) because of their excellent optical and chemical properties (Xiao and Sun, 2018). CDs synthesis and applications are greatly reported, especially the synthesis of CDs from plant parts are extensively studied (Perumal et al., 2021). Generally, the plants consist of many phytoconstituents such as alkaloids, flavonoids, glycosides, and so on which have heteroatoms (Naik et al., 2020). Thus, it was expected that heteroatoms in CDs will help in the adsorption of the heavy metal ions. CDs that are synthesized using natural sources are generally enriched with nitrogen atoms (Atchudan et al., 2018; Raji et al., 2019; Zulfajri et al., 2019; Tadesse et al., 2020). An increase in nitrogen content in hydrogel particles is expected to improve the HMI adsorption. Thus, herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was utilized for the preparation of hydrogel particles. N-CDs show promising applications in biology and chemistry fields. N-CDs themselves possess phytoconstituents from their natural sources (fruits, leaves, etc.) (Atchudan et al., 2018; Raji et al., 2019; Zulfajri et al., 2019; Tadesse et al., 2020). Here, N-CDs was prepared using red Malus floribunda (Red-MF-fruit) fruits and characterization of N-CDs reveals the presence of elements such as carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O). Using chitosan (C), gelatin (G), and prepared N-CDs, spherical hydrogel hybrid particles (CGCDs) were prepared by the inverse emulsion process. Nitrogen in CDs suggests the prepared CGCDs might enhance the adsorption of HMI. Adsorption of Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), and Hg(II) was performed using CGCDs in a single (S-HMI) and multiple metal ion (M-HMI) systems. Also, experiments were performed with different concentrations of HMI as 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L to confirm adsorption efficiency.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analysis was carried out using Hitachi S-4800 at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV for N-CDs. High-resolution transmittance electron microscopy images (HR-TEM) of N-CDs were performed with an FEI-Tecnai TF-20 transmission electron microscope with an operating accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of N-CDs were recorded in transmittance mode on a PerkinElmer Spectrum100 in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 by the addition of 8 scans at a resolution of 8 cm−1 at the core research support center for natural products and medical materials of Yeungnam University. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of N-CDs were recorded from 200 to 800 nm using an OPTIZEN 3220UV spectrophotometer. Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation wavelength was varied to determine the maximum emission intensity. The slit width was fixed at 5 nm and the scan speed was set to 400 nm/min.

The morphology of the prepared hydrogel particles (CGCDs) was visualized using an FE-SEM (15 kV, Hitachi SU8220, Japan) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) analyses were used to confirm the existence of metal ions on the CGCDs surface after the adsorption experiment. X-ray diffraction analyses (XRD) of graphene composites, chitosan, and gelatin were carried out at a scattering angle of 2θ in the range of 10–60° using a diffractometer (D/MAX-2500, Rigaku, Japan) with CuKα as the radiation source (λ = 1.5404 Å). To examine the functional groups in the hydrogels and to confirm the cross-linking reaction, ATR-FTIR (8400 S, Shimadzu, Japan) was used. Metal ion concentrations were measured using an inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Optima 7300 DV & Avio 500, PerkinElmer, U.S.A). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific), CasaXPS software was used for the deconvolution of the high-resolution XPS spectra.

The N-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal carbonization method using Red-MF-fruit and ammonium hydroxide solution as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. Briefly, Red-MF-fruit were thoroughly washed and grounded well with distilled water (DI). About 70 mL of fruit juice was taken in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, to this 2 mL ammonium hydroxide solution was added as a dopant (pH = 7–8). The autoclave was tightly closed, and hydrothermal treatment was done for 12 h at 200 °C. After the desired reaction time, the mixture was cooled and thus a yellowish-brown solution was obtained. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm mixed cellulose esters membrane to remove the unwanted or bigger particles or dust. The solution was subjected the vacuum freeze-drying that results in a brown solid and is used for further characterization. Scheme 1 elucidates the synthesis of N-CDs from Red-MF-fruit by a simple hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hybrid spherical hydrogel particles (CGCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Thoroughly characterized N-CDs were employed in the novel hybrid hydrogel particles (CGCDs) synthesis (Scheme 2 ). Spherical carbon dot hybrid hydrogel particles were prepared using chitosan, gelatin, and prepared N-CDs. 75 g of a hexane solution of Span 85 (5 wt%), 25 g of chitosan solution (3 wt%), 25 g of aq. gelatin solution (10 wt%), and 0.125 g of aq. N-CDs solution (0.5 wt%) was taken in a double jacket container. Span 85 was used as a surfactant, chitosan was dissolved in water using an acetic acid solution, and gelatin was dissolved in water by gentle warming. The temperature in a double jacket container was maintained at 45 °C by water circulation and the reaction mixture was stirred at 550 rpm for 2 h. After the desired time, dropwise addition of glutaraldehyde (25 g, 1 wt% aq. solution) was done and the stirring was continued for 30 min. To remove Span 85 which was stabilized on the CGCDs particles was washed thoroughly with hexane 5 times and then washed with DI water. The obtained CGCDs were then dried in a convective drying oven and stored for further use.

ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was performed to investigate the surface functionalization that exists on N-CDs. In the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3 a), a broad peak between 3450 and 3200 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –NH and –OH respectively, the peaks at 2927 and 2856 cm−1 are responsible for the asymmetric C–H stretching and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations, respectively. The existence of CO and CC groups in N-CDs is revealed from the peaks at 1715 and 1614 cm−1, respectively. The band at 1444 cm−1 is ascribed to C–N, the peaks around 1370 cm−1 and 1318 cm−1 are responsible for C–H and N–H bending vibrations, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020a). The presence of C–OH and C–O–C bonds are confirmed from the peaks around 1245 and 1057 cm−1, respectively (You et al., 2016). The peak around 780 cm−1 is attributed to –OH/–CH out-of-plane bending vibrations of alcohols (Ahmed et al., 2016). These results confirm the presence of functional groups such as –NH, C–H, C–N, CO, and CC in N-CDs. Fig. 3b shows the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs, a peak around 285 nm and a weak shoulder peak around 326 nm ascribed to π–π* transition of the sp2 carbon core and n–π* transition of CO/CN group, which can persuade to strong fluorescence, respectively (Zhang and Chen, 2014; Gao et al., 2016; Atchudan et al., 2018, 2019). The excitation spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs (Fig. 3c) shows a maximum intensity peak of around 340 nm. Optical images of the aqueous N-CD solution (inset of Fig. 3c) show the pale yellow and cyan color under daylight and the 365 nm of UV-light, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs exhibit an intense fluorescence peak at 411 nm when excited at the wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. S1). The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were measured by varying the excitation wavelength from 300 to 440 nm at 20 nm intervals, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3d. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity steadily increased with the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity increased from 300 nm to 340 nm and then gradually decreased with the bathochromic shift. The results confirm that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs depends on the excitation wavelength which generally depends on particle size and defects on the surface of the particles (Ju and Chen, 2014; Atchudan et al., 2020b; Senol and Bozkurt, 2020). Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is an important metric that quantifies the ability of a particle to release absorbed electromagnetic radiation as photons. The QY was calculated using Eq. (S5). Hydrothermally synthesized N-CDs from Red-MF-fruit have achieved a QY of 18 % and the obtained QY is acceptable for analytical applications.

Even though here the N-CDs were not used for the optical application towards HMI experiments. In general, the prolonging (long-term) and photobleaching stability of the N-CDs is important for true optical applications. The N-CDs aqueous solution was stored for a long time and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was measured at different time intervals. The comparison emission spectra of N-CDs on the 1st and 90th days were displayed as Fig. 4 a. Insignificant fluorescence intensity changes were observed even after storage for 90 days which is because of the presence of electrostatic repulsions between the N-CDs. This confirms the excellent prolonging stability of synthesized N-CDs. The digital photographs of the aq. solution of N-CDs shows the fluorescence emission under the 365 nm UV light at the time of synthesis (Day 1). This is retained even after 90 days of stored samples which are shown as inset Fig. 4a. The images displayed bright cyan/blue color under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm UV light. Notably, no manifest aggregation or fluorescence brightness changes in the N-CDs aqueous solution even after 90 days of storage were observed. This confirms that the N-CDs exhibit prolonging fluorescence stability. Further, the N-CDs aq. solution was irradiated under the 365 nm UV light (8 W) for 60 min to determine the photostability/photobleaching stability, and the fluorescence intensity was measured with 20 min intervals which are shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence result displays the insignificant changes in the fluorescence intensity of initial (0 min/before irradiation) and at irradiation of 60 min under strong UV light. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the N-CDs was examined by the naked eye view of the digital photos of fluorescence emission of N-CDs aqueous solution under the 365 nm UV light. The inset in Fig. 4b displays the digital photos of N-CDs aq. solution before and after irradiation under UV light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. This result suggests that the prepared N-CDs have very good prolonging stability and photostability.

Using the synthesized N-CDs, spherical CGCDs hydrogel particles were prepared along with C and G. The prepared CGCDs were characterized by various techniques. Fig. 5 a shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of chitosan, gelatin, N-CDs, and CGCDs. Chitosan spectra reveal the characteristic bands around 3360 and 3290 cm−1 which are responsible for the –NH and –OH stretching, respectively (Negrea et al., 2015; Badawy et al., 2017). The C–H, asymmetric and symmetric stretching observed around 2920 and 2860 cm−1, respectively. The presence of CO stretching confirms the band around 1650 cm−1. The amino groups from chitosan and gelatin are deformed by the crosslinking with glutaraldehyde which is clear from the band around 1580 cm−1. The absorption peaks between 1150 and 900 cm−1 confirm the existence of glucose units that are present in the structure of chitosan. O–H and C–H stretching of gelatin show the adsorption bands around 3290 and 2940 cm−1, respectively (Irfanita et al., 2017). CO stretching, N–H bending, and in the plane of vibrations C–N and N–H groups or vibrations of CH2 groups of side chains are denoted as Amide-I, amide-II, and amide-III, are appearing around 1620, 1560, and 1220 cm−1, respectively.

For better understanding and comparison, the N-CDs spectrum from Fig. 3a is repeated in Fig. 5. In CGCDs, the –NH from chitosan and gelatin structures are observed around 3489 cm-1 –OH stretching bands from N-CDs, chitosan, and gelatin were overlapped and observed around 3298 cm−1 in CGCDs. Overlapping of C–H stretching from chitosan, gelatin, and N-CDs together appears around 2919 cm−1. The absorption band around 1740 cm−1 is responsible for CO stretching, the bands around 1630, and 1510 cm−1 are attributed to CC and N–H stretching, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020b). In Fig. 5a the presence of absorption bands of N-CDs, gelatin, and chitosan in the spectrum of CGCDs confirms the successful formation of CGCDs. Fig. 5b shows the XRD spectra of chitosan, gelatin, N-CDs, and prepared CGCDs. Chitosan shows a peak around 19.9°, which corresponds to an amorphous chitosan polymeric structure (Rhim et al., 2006). The triple-helical crystalline structure of gelatin shows a peak around 19.8° (Das et al., 2017). A peak at 2θ = 21° in N-CDs can be indexed to C (002) plane. This revealed the graphitic nature of N-CDs (Atchudan et al., 2018). In CGCDs, a broad peak around 2θ = 20.7° suggests the merge peaks from chitosan, gelatin, and N-CDs. Appearance/merge of peaks from chitosan, gelatin, and N-CDs in CGCDs inform the successful preparation of CGCDs.

The morphology of the prepared CGCDs was confirmed using FE-SEM measurements. Fig. 6 reveals the FE-SEM images of CGCDs at different magnifications. The hydrogel particles are spherical, and the sizes were measured between 20 μm and 300 μm. The cross-sectional image shows the pores inside the particles (Fig. 6d). The DS% of prepared CGCDs was calculated as about 1320% using the Eq. (S1). This DS% is ~1.5 times higher than the DS% of hydrogels with and without graphene oxide (Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). Implies the incorporation of N-CDs in hydrogel particles, impacting the swelling degree. Thus, we believed that the higher DS% of CGCDs might enhance the HMI adsorption.

The prepared CGCDs were used to adsorbs HMI such as Pb(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), and Hg(II). In our recent work (Perumal et al., 2019, 2020), 1000 mg/L of HMI was used for the adsorption experiments. To confirm the adsorption efficiency of HMI with low and higher concentrations than our previous reports, here low (500 mg/L) and high (1500 mg/L) HMI concentrations were included along with 1000 mg/L concentration (Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). Therefore, here three concentrations of HMI were used (500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L), and using ICP-OES measurements, the actual concentrations of the HMI stock solutions were confirmed. Table 1 shows the resemblance of an increase in actual concentration with an increase in stock solution concentration. These known concentration solutions of HMI were used for the adsorption experiments. Adsorption experiments were carried out in S-HMI and M-HMI systems. In M-HMI adsorption systems, equal amounts of M-HMI solutions were used.

Fig. 7 shows the morphology of the CGCDs using FE-SEM, after the adsorption of Hg(II) and Pb(II) ions in S-HMI systems. Fig. 7a shows the overview of the CGCDs images with Hg(II) ions on the surface of CGCDs, confirming the spherical shape of CGCDs and the retainment of the spherical shape after HMI adsorption. The magnified SEM images of CGCDs after Hg(II) ion adsorption reveals the rough surface or contamination on the CGCDs surface as compared to as-prepared CGCDs (Fig. 7a&b). Therefore, the rough or contaminated surface as shown in Fig. 7 is considered to be brought about by the adsorption of Hg(II) ions on the CGCD surface. The FE-SEM images (Fig. 7c&d) of CGCDs after Pb(II) adsorption display the smooth surface of CGCDs which suggests a small amount of Pb(II) on CGCDs. Fig. S2 shows the FE-SEM images of CGCD particles after Cd(II) and Cr(III) adsorptions. The smooth surfaces of hydrogel particles suggest the lowest or minor amount of Cd(II) and Cr(III) ions are adsorbed by CGCDs. The adsorption experiment was carried out using just with N-CDs and Fig. S3a depicts the surface morphology and elemental mapping of N-CDs after the adsorption of Hg(II) with 500 mg/L in S-HMI. After the adsorption of the Hg(II) ion, smooth surfaces of N-CDs were observed as that observed before adsorption experiments (Fig. 1). Fig. S3 (b-e) reveals the existence of C, O, N, and Hg(II) ions on N-CDs surface after adsorption. However, the surface of CGCDs after adsorption (Fig. 7) of HMI was rougher than the surface before metal ion adsorption (Fig. 6). This because the adsorption of Hg(II) is higher on CGCDs than on N-CDs surface. This suggests that CGCDs are efficient HMI adsorbents than compared to N-CDs.

The adsorption of HMI by CGCDs was further analyzed by ICP-OES measurements. The non-equilibrium distribution coefficients (K d), removal efficiency (%), and absorption capacity (Qe) values were calculated using Eqs (S2), (S3), and (S4), respectively. The results after HMI adsorption are shown in Table 2 . The K d value ranges between 2.00*10−3 and 0.26 mL/g, removal efficiency lies between 99.0 % and 0.31 %, and absorption capacity (Qe) showed calculated values between 90.0 mg/g and 0.033 mg/g. Further, Table 2 reveals that the K d and removal efficiency (%) tendency as 500 mg/L > 1000 mg/L > 1500 mg/L. In the S-HMI adsorption system, Hg(II) ion shows a high value of K d and removal efficiency. Maximum removal efficiency (92 %) of Hg(II) ions was calculated by CGCDs using Hg(II) 500 mg/L stock solution. Using CGCDs, the removal efficiency of Cd(II) shows zero value with the concentrations of 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L Cd(II) stock solution. 500 mg/L Cr(III) solution showed about 0.31 % removal efficiency, while 1000 mg/L and 1500 mg/L showed zero adsorption of Cr(III). The higher K d, Qe, and % values of Hg(II) ions than other metal ions are because of the greater affinity of Hg(II) ions towards nitrogen atoms that exist in CGCDs. To check the effect of N-CDs on the adsorption of HMI, just N-CDs were utilized for the adsorption experiments. As Hg(II) ion showed higher removal efficiency than compared to other metal ions, only Hg(II) adsorption using N-CDs in S-HMI was measured. As mentioned in Table 2, the K d, Qe, and % values of Hg(II) ions using N-CDs are much lower than those measured using CGCDs. 92.0% removal efficiency of Hg(II) was observed using CGCDs while N-CDs showed only 53 %. This suggests that just N-CDs will not impact on adsorption of HMI. Thus, because of the low adsorption of Hg(II) ion on N-CDs, the surface morphology of N-CDs before and after metal ion adsorption (Fig. 1 and Fig. S3) are the same.

We also performed XPS measurements to confirm the adsorption of HMI to the CGCDs surface. The ICP-OES results reveal higher adsorption results using 500 mg/L concentration of HMI than using 1000 mg/L and 1500 mg/L concentrations. Thus, XPS measurements were done for the samples where adsorption experiments were carried out using 500 mg/L of HMI. Fig. S4 survey spectra of CGCDs before and after adsorption experiment (CGCDs-Hg, CGCDs-Cd in S-HMI and CGCDs-Mix in M-HMI). Fig. S4 reveals the presence of elements C, N, and O on CGCDs, further Fig. S4 exposes the intensity of N in CGCDs is low compared to N in CGCDs-Hg, CGCDs-Cd, and CGCDs-Mix. This is attributed to the involvement of the N group in CGCDs with HMI in the adsorption experiment. Fig. S5 depicts the high resolution spectra of CGCDs. The deconvolution of C 1s reveals (Fig. S5a) four peaks at 284.8, 286.4, 287.7, and 288.9 eV which corresponds to C–C/C–H, C–N/C–O/C–OH, CO, and OC–OH/CN, respectively. The high resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. S5b) discloses two peaks, C–O/CO and OC–OH at 532.3 and 533.4 eV, respectively. N 1 S spectra depicted as Fig. S5c show three peaks which are attributed to C–N, N–H, and C3–N observed at 399.0, 400.0, and 402.0, eV respectively.

As highest and lowest values of K d, Qe, and % were obtained for the adsorption of Hg(II) and Cd(II) ions, respectively, thus XPS studies were done only for CGCDs after adsorption of Hg(II) and Cd(II) ions. Fig. 8 depicts the high resolution spectra of CGCDs-Hg after adsorption of Hg(II) with 500 mg/L using CGCDs in the S-HMI system. The high resolution spectra of C 1s level (Fig. 8a), reveals four peaks at 284.8, 285.8, 286.7, and 289.0 eV which are attributed to C–C/C–H, C–N, C–O/C–OH, and OC–OH/CN, respectively. Deconvolution of O 1s (Fig. 8b) showed two peaks that correspond to C–O/CO and OC–OH at 532.4 and 533.6 eV, respectively. Three peaks were observed by deconvolution of N 1s (Fig. 8c) at 398.5, 400.5, 401.8 eV responsible for C–N, N–H, and C3–N/N–Hg, respectively. The peak at 401.8 eV which corresponds to C3–N from N-CDs is responsible for Hg(II) ion interaction with the nitrogen group in CGCDs. Further, the interaction between CGCDs and metal ions confirms from the slight lower shifts of peaks in N 1s level and higher shifts of peaks in O 1s level in CGCDs-Hg than compared to N 1s and O 1s levels of CGCDs. Fig. S6 illustrates the XPS spectra of CGCDs after Cd(II) adsorption using 500 mg/L in the S-HMI system. The absence of Cd 3 d peak in XPS spectra of CGCDs (S-HMI system) confirms the 0 % removal efficiency of Cd(II) using CGCDs. Fig. S6 reveals the high resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. S6a), O 1s (Fig. S6b), and N 1s (Fig. S6c). The deconvolution of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s levels results in four, two, and three peaks, respectively. The peaks in N 1s and O 1s levels of CGCDs-Cd and CGCDs were observed at the same energy levels this suggests no adsorption of Cd(II) ion using CGCDs. This result supports the ICP-OES and SEM results.

Fig. 9 a and b shows the FE-SEM images of CGCDs with M-HMI and their elemental mapping (Fig. 9c–h) images. As explained in the before section for the S-HMI system, the images confirm the spherical shape of CGCDs after the adsorption of HMI in the M-HMI system.

Fig. 10 shows the FE-SEM images of just N-CDs after HMI (500 mg/L) adsorption. Further, Fig. 10 reveals the existence of Hg(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), and Cr(III) ions on N-CDs surface after the adsorption experiment. The smooth surface of N-CDs maintenance after the adsorption experiment (Fig. 10a). The even distribution of C, N, O, Hg(II), Pb(II), Cr(III), and Cd(II) on N-CDs surface (Fig. 10b–h) confirms the adsorption of HMI using N-CDs. However, the intensity of HMI on N-CDs surface (Fig. 10) is less than the intensity of HMI on CGCDs (Fig. 9). This shows consistent with the ICP-OES result.

The K d, Qe, and % values of Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III) ions using N-CDs are relatively lower than those measured using CGCDs. This implies just N-CDs or CG will not show the efficient result as that observed for CGCDs particles. In addition, this confirms that functional groups from CGCDs are involved efficiently in the adsorption of HMI. The low K d, Qe, and % values using N-CDs than CGCDs show consistent results with SEM measurements. Further, the adsorption of HMI on CGCDs surface was investigated using XPS. Fig. S4 depicts the survey spectrum of CGCDs-Mix which confirms the existence of C, N, and O elements, because of the low concentrations of HMI there are not evident from survey spectra. However, the high resolution spectra confirm the presence of HMI. Fig. 11 , reveals the high resolution XPS spectra of CGCDs after HMI adsorption experiments. Fig. 11a depicts the high resolution spectra of C 1s level shows four peaks on deconvolution at 284.8, 285.8, 286.7, and 289.0 eV which are attributes to C–C/C–H, C–N, C–O/C–OH, and OC–OH/CN, respectively. The energy levels of C–N, C–O/C–OH, and OC–OH/CN in CGCDs-Mix were slight shifts to the lower energy than compared to CGCDs. This is because of the coordination of C–N, C–O/C–OH, and OC–OH/CN groups in CGCDs with HMI. The high resolution of O 1s level on deconvolution shows two peaks that are responsible for C–O/CO and OC-ON that are observed at 532.3 and 533.6 eV, respectively. C–O/CO group in CGCDs-Mix and CGCDs were observed at the same energy level (532.3 eV). However, the energy level of OC–OH in CGCDs-Mix (533.6 eV) showed a slight shift compared to CGCDs, suggest that OC–OH is involved in the interaction with HMI. N 1s level shows three peaks at 399.6, 400.5, 402.5 eV which are attributes to C–N, N–H, and N-M+/C3–N, also these peaks in CGCDs-Mix slightly shifts to a higher energy than compared to CGCDs. This suggests the strong involvement of the N atom in CGCDs with HMI. Also the high resolution of Hg4f, Pb4f, Cd3d, and Cr2p shows two peaks each at 102.2 and 105.5 eV, 138.7 and 143.8 eV, 405.3 and 412.08, 575.4 and 581.5 eV which are corresponds to Hg4f7/2 and Hg4f5/2, Pb4f7/2 and Pb4f5/2, Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2, and Cd3d5/2 and Cd3d3/2, respectively. The overall XPS studies support ICP-OES and SEM results.

Thus all studies confirm that the introduction of N-CDs into hydrogel particles improves the adsorption efficiency of HMI when compared to reported hydrogel particles (Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). The nitrogen atoms in N-CDs are responsible for enhancing the HMI adsorptions. CGCDs showed a maximum of 92 % of Hg(II) ion in the S-HMI system. 99 % of Hg(II) ions were removed efficiently by CGCDs in M-HMI systems. For the Pb(II) and Cr(III) ions, CGCDs showed more than 80 % adsorption. More than 75 % of Cd(II) ion is removed by CGCDs in M-HMI systems, while 0 % of Cd(II) ion was adsorbed by CGCDs in the S-HMI system. This is because, in multiple systems, all-metal ion competes to adsorb HMI on the CGCDs surface, promoting the adsorption of all metal ions. Thus, CGCDs showed >70 % removal efficiency of all metal ions. However, in the M-HMI, changes in metal ion concentrations do not impact the HMI adsorption efficiency.

The possible interactions involved in the HMI adsorption are depicted in Fig. 12 . As illustrated in Fig. 12, the functional groups such as amine, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl in CGCDs involved in the interaction with HMI. The presence of several functional groups suggests the electrostatic interactions between CGCDs and HMI. Apart from functional groups present in C and G, functional groups in N-CDs are also involved in the interaction with metal ions. Thus higher removal efficiency values were observed using CGCDs than reported ones (Perumal et al., 2019, 2020). In the S-HMI system, the removal efficiency of Hg(II) by CGCDs is efficient than other metal ions. This can be for many reasons, Hg(II) ion shows a strong affinity towards nitrogen groups (Monier and Abdel-Latif, 2012; Perumal et al., 2019, 2020) in CGCDs rather than other metal ions. This can be explained by the concept of hard-soft acid-base (HSAB) theory. The nitrogen group will show a strong affinity towards the soft acid Hg(II) ions (Lone et al., 2019). Controversy results were observed in the M-HMI systems that regardless of metal ions, all-metal ions showed high removal efficiency values (>70 %). The conceivable reasons are as follows, in multiple systems, all-metal ions try to participate in the adsorption on CGCDs surface. Thus all metal ions might strive to interact with nitrogen atoms that exist in CGCDs. Also, the electronegativities of metal ions, Hg(II), Cr(III), Pb(II), and Cd(II) were reported as 2.0, 1.8, 1.7, and 1.6, respectively (Sitko et al., 2013). This electronegativity of metal ions tendency show consistent with the tendency of removal efficiency values of metal ions by CGCDs. The high removal efficiency of Hg(II) with CGCDs as Hg(II) ion has a high electronegativity value as compared to other metal ions. Cd(II) removal efficiency is less as it has the least electronegativity value. As the differences in electronegativity values of Pb(II) and Cr(III) were nearly identical, almost the same removal efficiency values of these metal ions using CGCDs were observed. Further, the adsorption of metal ions using CGCDs can be explained with the pH of the solution. Here the pH of the metal ion solution was not altered or maintained instead the metal ion solution was used as it. The prepared metal ion solution itself showed different pH values, like pH = 4 for Cr(III) and Pb(II) ion solutions, pH = 5 for Hg(II) ion solution, and pH = 6 for Cd(II) solution. Thus metal ion solutions with different pH values might affect the interaction between metal ions and CGCDs surface. In S-HMI, Hg(II) ions solution which had pH = 5 showed maximum removal efficiency results. Whereas Cr(III) and Cd(II) ion solution with pH = 4 showed low removal efficiency values. This suggests at pH = 4, the amine groups in CGCDs particle will be protonated and will bear a positive charge on it (Jin and Bai, 2002). Thus it is believed that this positive charge on CGCDs will repel the metal ion and will not allow the interactions between CGCDs surface and HMI. Also, unfortunately, Cd(II) showed 0 % removal efficiency as the pH of Cd(II) solution was about pH = 6. It is reported that an increase in pH of Cd(II) solution decrease the removal efficiency because Cd(OH)2 will be a dominant species at pH > 6.0 and Cd2+ and Cd(OH)+ will be dominant at pH less than 6.0 (Elkhatib et al., 2016). In this work pH was measured as pH = 6, this suggests the presence of Cd(OH)2 species which will precipitate, and thus Cd(II) ions do not adsorb using CGCDs. However, in M-HMI pH was measured as pH = 5 which eases the adsorption of HMI using CGCDs. Thus maximum removal efficiency of Hg(II) was observed at pH = 5, this reveals that pH = 5 is an optimal condition to obtain good removal efficiency results.

Among 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L, the metal ion concentrations with 500 mg/L showed high adsorption. The facts involved can be explained as follows: the functional groups such as amine, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl that exist in CGCDs particles were responsible for the HMI adsorption by interaction. Once these functional groups in CGCDs are involved in the interaction with HMI. Further interaction is not possible between HMI and CGCDs at high concentrations because of the unavailable active sites in CGCDs. Thus, an increase in HMI concertation leads to a decrease in metal ion adsorption. This suggests that 500 mg/L concentration of HMI is an optimum concentration to get better adsorption results by CGCDs hydrogel particles.

Comparison of the obtained results with literature reported values from publication (Monier and Abdel-Latif, 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Astrini et al., 2015; Fato et al., 2019; Lone et al., 2019; Perumal et al., 2019, 2020; Lessa et al., 2020) are shown in Table 3 . In this table, the removal efficiency of metal ions by different adsorbents was tabulated. Comparing the obtained results with reported ones is difficult since different adsorbent materials are utilized and different experimental conditions are maintained. However, the obtained removal efficiency results of M-HMI using CGCDs showed relatively high values than the reported ones. The removal efficiency of Hg(II) ion using CGCDs showed >70 % while reported adsorbents, gelatin-chitosan hydrogel showed removal efficiency of Hg(II) about 97 %. Chitosan-phenylthiourea resin showed removal efficiency of Hg(II) of about 79.4 %. Thus the synthesized CGCDs hydrogel particles will be a suitable candidate for the simultaneous removal of multiple metal ions and can be utilized for practical applications.

N-CDs were prepared using Red-MF-fruit. Subsequently, the prepared N-CDs were used to prepare spherical CGCDs hydrogel particles by the inverse emulsion method. The prepared N-CDs and CGCDs were characterized by various physicochemical techniques. The spherical-shaped N-CDs were measured with 4.0 nm in diameter and the presence of elements such as nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon were confirmed by ATR-FTIR, SEM-EDX, and XPS Studies. In addition, UV–visible and fluorescence measurements confirm the acceptable optical properties. The prepared CGCDs showed a high DS% value. This high DS% value (1320 %) of CGCDs suggests using the prepared CGCDs for HMI adsorption. In a single system, CGCDs showed maximum adsorption of Hg(II) with 92 % using 500 mg/L of Hg(II) stock solution. In multiple systems, CGCDs showed almost complete removal of Hg(II) with 99 % using 500 mg/L of Hg(II) ion stock solution. Furthermore, in multiple systems, all metal ions showed removal efficiency >70 %. The prepared CGCDs showed promising results in the removal of HMI which are correlates with high DS%, enriched heteroatoms, pH of HMI solutions, electronegativities of HMI, and affinity towards between functionalities in CGCDs and HMI. In addition, the adsorption of HMI on CGCDs was confirmed from XPS studies. A slight shift in energy levels of N 1s and O 1s confirms the involvement of functional groups from CGCDs in the interaction with HMI. Further, the studies-SEM, ICP-OES, and XPS showed consistent results. Therefore, the prepared CGCDs can be directly employed in practical use for the simultaneous removal of M-HMI from wastewater.
